g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Turing Machines, the foundation of computational theory.
A Turing Machine is an abstract computational model with infinite tape, a read-write head, and finite control.
g.text: An abstract computational model that consists of:

g.data-narration: Alan Turing introduced this model in 1936 to solve the Entscheidungsproblem, establishing the limits of computation.
g.text: Alan Turing's paper "On Computable Numbers" solved Hilbert's Entscheidungsproblem
Establishes theoretical limits of what computers can compute

g.data-narration: A TM is formally defined as a 7-tuple with states, alphabets, transition function, and special states.
g.text: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject)

g.data-narration: The four essential components are the infinite tape, read-write head, control unit, and state register.
The transition function determines the next state, symbol to write, and head movement direction.
The TM starts at q₀, reads input, follows transitions, and halts when entering accept or reject states.

g.text: Start at q₀, head at leftmost cell
Read symbol, apply δ(q, a) = (p, b, d)
Write b, move head direction d
If TM never reaches accept/reject → loops forever (doesn't halt)

g.data-narration: Turing-recognizable languages can be accepted by some TM; decidable languages halt on all inputs.

g.text: Rejects OR loops on strings not in L
Rejects ALL strings not in L (always halts)

g.data-narration: The Church-Turing Thesis states any computable function can be computed by a TM - the foundation of CS theory.
g.text: can be computed by a Turing Machine"
This is a THESIS (accepted, not proven) - relates formal definition to intuitive notion of algorithm

g.data-narration: Multi-tape and non-deterministic TMs are computationally equivalent to the basic single-tape deterministic TM.

g.text: Same power as DTM, related to P vs NP

g.data-narration: Example: TM for {0ⁿ1ⁿ} replaces each 0 with X and 1 with Y to verify equal counts.
g.text: Replace each 0→X, each 1→Y in left-to-right manner

g.data-narration: Beyond language acceptance, TMs can compute functions by writing output to tape before halting.
g.text: Unlike language acceptors, computing TMs produce output on the tape before halting
This capability makes TMs Turing-complete - equivalent in power to any real computer

g.data-narration: Key points: TM is a 7-tuple with infinite tape, more powerful than FA and PDA, Church-Turing Thesis.

g.text: TM = 7-tuple M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject)
Infinite tape distinguishes TM from Finite Automata and Pushdown Automata
TM recognizes recursively enumerable languages - the broadest class in Chomsky hierarchy
Church-Turing Thesis: TM = standard model of computation
All variants (multi-tape, NTM) are computationally equivalent to basic TM

g.data-narration: Remember: Reject ≠ Loop! Decidable languages always halt. Church-Turing is a thesis, not a theorem.

g.text: • Confusing "reject" with "loop forever" - they are different!
• Blank symbol B ∈ Γ but B ∉ Σ
• Church-Turing is a thesis, not a theorem - cannot be proven formally
• Understand r.e. vs recursive language difference
